context.description: A philosophical nonfiction passage develops the idea of "two-track awareness" by distinguishing awareness from attention. It contrasts outward and inward attention and argues that awareness can encompass both the speaker and the surrounding situation at once.
normalization_notes: Translated German section heading into English for retrieval text.
No exact chapter boundary is visible; classified as theoretical discussion within book body content.

rows.paragraph_text: Reflective questions ask how awareness can expand beyond limited self-awareness and open access to new information and possibilities.
The section "Two-track awareness" distinguishes awareness from attention, arguing that attention can be directed outward or inward while awareness can encompass the whole situation.
It presents awareness as capable of simultaneously registering the speaker, the conversation, and the surrounding environment without being narrowed by a single aim.

content.free_text: book_page book_body_content theoretical_discussion Book page on awareness and attention. Section: Two-track awareness. Topics: awareness, attention, consciousness, inner and outer attention, two-track awareness.
